replies.author: @TheZitherish
replies.text: I have made turbines using feathers. It’s tricky to find equal size and shape and they have to be only from either port or starboard wing. Interestingly, I have found Rook feathers to be the most durable in the long term, others seem to rot away.

author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
text: Interesting stuff, which brought me to  question in my mind. Each feather is basically made up of individual splines, so how do they “bind” together in the turbulence of air during flight without damaging the splines?

replies.author: @offgridmanpolktn
replies.text:
The staggered connection to the skin and layers interlocking, make the strength and muscle control let's then flex the individual feathers for extension and angle control.  So any barb connections are also under their control.
If you research neuro studies a big part of their brains is flight control, pretty cool huh?
Peace

replies.author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
replies.text: @offgridmanpolktn  Yes thats the feather as a whole, what Im referring to is the individual parts of the feather that make it up.

replies.author: @offgridmanpolktn
replies.text:
​@Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
   Okay, there is the main shaft of the feather 
   Then hundreds ( thousands?), of tiny shafts off each side of the main one. Then the barbs you refer to are on the ends of even tinier that makes the tiny tiny side shaft's stay cohesive in a line.
  Seems you might have played with a feather to make the edge ripple over your finger. Or made it split in the side and brush it back together by brushing your fingers up it 
    Now it is still possible to do this when it's dead and dried out. When that feather is alive it is connected blood flow, tissue connection to skin and cartilage, and nerve connection it's a growing living part of a bird that they can feel and control.
   So when birds flap their wings they control feather movement, the almost microscopic barbs allow side shaft flexibility 
Better explanation?
✌️

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they do get damaged mate - which is why birds preen. They run their beaks through the splines which 're-zips them' so to speak

replies.author: @dekutree64
replies.text: @offgridmanpolktn Feathers are only filled with blood while growing. Once complete, the blood drains out and it's a non-living structure, like a fingernail, but far more elaborate. Most birds grow a new set every year, as they do wear out from constant use.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate
